data.title: Upexi: Offering Registration Likely Limits Upside (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in May, I initiated coverage of Upexi, Inc (NASDAQ:UPXI) and called the stock a 'sell' due to its inflated valuation relative to the combo of its legacy business and its Solana (SOL-USD) themed DAT strategy. Since that 'sell' call, UPXI shares have fallen by as much as 70%. Currently, UPXI shares are just 40% off that mid-May sell call:

Given the fact that SOL is actually up 5% over that same stretch, I think it would be fair to call UPXI's performance as a SOL-proxy considerably poor over these last 5 months. As I said at the end of that May piece, good assets can be purchased at a bad price. And even though Upexi paid a great price for its SOL earlier this year, $10 for UPXI in May was a bad deal. At a price currently under $6, prospects look a lot better.

Solana Strategy and mNAV

Just as (Micro)Strategy (MSTR) had been able to do for the last several years, Upexi quickly scaled up its Solana holdings through various financial instruments like common stock and convertible note issuance. According to data tracked by Blockworks Research, Solana DATs hold roughly 15.8 million SOL combined.

Of that combined SOL position through DAT companies, Upexi holds a little over 2 million SOL and is the fourth largest Solana-themed DAT in the market. The table below breaks down the value of Upexi's Solana relative to its current market capitalization and its post-share registration diluted market capitalization:

mNAV Calculation Prior To Offering

Offering Completion
Shares 58,888,756 94,457,980
UPXI Price $5.61 $5.61
Solana 2,018,419 2,018,419
Solana Price $185 $185
Solana Value Per Share $6.34 $3.95
mNAV 0.88 1.42

Source: Upexi, Analyst's Calculation, $185 SOL price at article submission

Utilizing a basic calculation alone, UPXI arguably looks cheap relative to its SOL holdings based on a $5.61 closing price and a $185 SOL price on October 16th. Things get a bit more murky due to the recent offering registration that could see as many as 48 million shares sold from buyers of Upexi's PIPE and convertible note. The problem for UPXI shareholders is the proceeds from the offered shares go to the selling stockholders rather than the company.

Thus, this would be expansion of the float that wouldn't lead to SOL purchases for UPXI shareholders. Not included in that offering is roughly 5 million in additional shares through options, warrants, and other instruments:

Excluded From Share Count UPXI Shares
Options ($3.39 ex) 621,353
Warrants ($4.20 ex) 1,848,735
Debt Converts 186,667
Preferred Converts 138,889
RSU from Incentive Plan 2,250,000
Total 5,045,644

Source: Upexi

Given those shares, we can calculate a fully diluted mNAV for UPXI at closer to 1.5. The justification for a fully diluted mNAV premium this large is debatable, in my view. The company has the ability to raise capital through its $500 million equity line. Raising capital in this way makes sense in a world where mNAV premium is extended. But I suspect as long as the stock trades at a discount to basic mNAV, diluting existing shareholders through that $500 million equity line is less likely. Especially considering such a large cohort of shareholders have registered their shares for sale, including CEO Allan Marshall - though he would retain the majority of his equity.

As I see it, Upexi's immediate path to acquiring more SOL is likely limited to its staking strategy.

In Upexi's last earnings report, the company noted an 8% annualized yield from staking SOL. This yield has come down quite a bit since the end of Upexi's last reported quarter. Investors can think of the relation between staked assets and staking yields similarly to the way they would bonds or dividends; the more demand there is for yield, the lower the yield goes.

Upexi's Staked SOL 2,018,419
Annual Yield as of 10/16 6.5%
Annualized Return at $185 $24,271,488

Source: Analyst's calculations

The good news is even at the current annualized SOL staking yield of 6.5%, 2 million staked SOL at a value of $185 per coin generates over $24 million in staking rewards. This would actually flip Upexi to a profitable company, all else being equal.

Legacy Business

It's important to remember that even though the company's equity valuation is now largely tied to the daily performance of Solana, Upexi continues to operate its legacy consumer product business. Even with roughly $1 million in digital asset revenue during the year ended June, Upexi still saw top line revenue decline year over year by 43% and lost $13.7 million during the fiscal year:

$9.8 million of Upexi's $11.2 million decline in revenue year over year was due to its divesting of its re-commerce business. Still, this implies that even adjusting for the re-commerce portion, Upexi's owned-brand consumer product business looks to have contributed roughly $1.4 million to that year over year revenue decline. The point is, if Upexi continues to operate its consumer business, it could come at the expense of SOL holdings in the future either through liquidated staking rewards or capital raised through equity offerings going toward opex.

Closing Thoughts

As I see it, the potential headwind of 48 million shares coming into the float without leading to direct SOL purchases limits the upside of the stock. At a basic mNAV discount, Upexi theoretically has an incentive to sell Solana and buy back common shares if doing so could generate greater returns than the staking yield from the SOL. But I largely view such a strategy as unlikely. My expectation is UPXI will perform in correlation with SOL for the foreseeable future, but may continue to lag the underlying in the event large quantities of the registered shares are sold. I'll cautiously upgrade UPXI to a 'hold' due to the possibility that basic mNAV could re-rate from a discount to a premium in the event SOL surges in price. But I still don't personally have any interest in buying shares of UPXI at this point in time.

data.title: SEIV: Deep Value, Differentiated Sector Mix
data.description:
With low valuations and a balanced market cap exposure, the SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Value Factor ETF (BATS:SEIV) is a value-oriented fund with a twist: a sector exposure that overweights technology and communication services, unlike most funds in the category. Still a relatively new fund, the combination of attractive valuation and strong returns warrants investors' attention. The coming sections will assess SEIV’s portfolio composition, valuation, and performance, stacking it up against the broader market and value peers.

ETF Description & Highlights

SEIV aims for long-term total returns through the investment in U.S. companies trading below their fair value, according to SEI Investments’ methodology. The fund adopts a fundamental approach, which consists of a rules-based stock selection framework that screens companies using factors like earnings, cash flow, sales, and typical valuation multiples, including price-to-earnings. The fund may also incorporate into this process additional metrics such as momentum, quality, and low volatility.

As of October 9, 2025, SEIV had nearly $887 million in assets under management, invested across 117 companies, with an average market cap of $68.4 billion. While the fund’s name highlights its large-cap focus, in reality, its portfolio looks more balanced across the capitalization spectrum than traditional benchmarks, like the Russell 1000. As such, SEIV has a 39.3% spread across large and mega caps, with mid caps accounting for 37.5% and small caps for 22.1%. By comparison, the Russell 1000 has 43.2% in mega caps and 32.2% in large caps, leaving less than 35% for mid and small caps.

SEIV’s top ten holdings are Nvidia (3.58%), Microsoft (3.40%), Apple (3.17%), Alphabet C (2.77%), Hewlett Packard Enterprise (2.68%), Alphabet A (2.49%), AT&T (2.37%), Micron (2.27%), Verizon (2.17%), and Cisco (2.16%), which together represent 27% of total assets. This list underscores a strong tilt toward technology and communication services, where names like HPE, Cisco, Micron, AT&T, and Verizon are all trading below 17x P/E.

The following table brings a mix of value-oriented funds with a broad exposure to the equity market. This list will serve as the peer group for SEIV in the analysis. The first two, the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) and the iShares Core S&P U.S. Value ETF (IUSV), are “classic benchmark” members of the group. Both follow cap-weighted indexes, Russell 1000 Value and S&P U.S. Value, respectively, and hold diversified portfolios of large- and mid-cap value U.S. stocks. Next, the Dimensional U.S. Marketwide Value ETF (DFUV) is actively managed and rooted in an optimization model that focuses on book-to-market and profitability factors. Meanwhile, the Vanguard U.S. Value Factor ETF (VFVA) uses a rules-based, actively managed strategy that screens for U.S. companies trading below intrinsic value and with a small- and micro-cap tilt. The WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund (WTV) emphasizes shareholder yield, blending dividends and buybacks as parameters to identify value. Finally, the WisdomTree U.S. AI Enhanced Value Fund (AIVL) adopts a machine-learning model to select stocks that appear undervalued.

SEIV’s largest sector is technology, accounting for 30.8% of the fund, followed by financial services (18.2%), communication services (14.4%), and healthcare (11.7%). Smaller weights include consumer discretionary (10.6%) and consumer staples (5.1%), while minor allocations are found in industrials (3.3%), utilities (3.3%), energy (1.6%), basic materials (0.6%), and real estate (0.4%). Relative to the Russell 1000, SEIV is overweight in financial services (+5.1%), communication services (+4.4%), and healthcare (+2.5%), offset by an underweight allocation to industrials (-5.1%) and technology (-3.7%).

The fund's stance on technology is quite significant for a value-oriented fund, with about 20 holdings spread across several segments. In hardware & storage, the fund owns Apple, HPE, NetApp, and Dell Technologies, making it the largest technology segment, unlike most funds, where semiconductors and software make up the bulk of the sector. Yet, holdings in Nvidia, Micron, and Qualcomm make semiconductors account for about 7% of the whole fund, while software remains with 4% through holdings in Microsoft, Zoom Video, and Five9.

In financial services, the fund has a meaningful exposure to insurance players like AIG, Unum, and Prudential, and also to reinsurance companies such as RenaissanceRe, AXIS Capital, and Assured Guaranty. Regional banks are also significant for the fund, with shares of Citizens Financial, Popular, Zions, and Bank OZK, while Synchrony Financial, Ally Financial, and OneMain add further exposure to the consumer finance segment.

The fund’s third largest sector, communication services, is focused on two pillars. The first is Alphabet, with more than 5% weight. The other one includes telecommunication service providers AT&T and Verizon, alongside broadband and media provider Comcast.

Outside the top three sectors, SEIV’s most meaningful positions include Cardinal Health and McKesson (drug distribution), Pfizer (big-cap pharma), Gilead Sciences (biotechnology), General Motors and Ford (auto industry), Delta Air Lines, United Airlines, American Airlines (airlines), Kroger (grocery), Exelon and FirstEnergy (regulated utilities), as well as APA, Valero, and Marathon Petroleum (energy exposure). Together, these names add a defensive sleeve to the fund with significant positioning in mature and relatively stable operations compared to the overall market.

SEIV’s heavy allocation to technology and communication services represents a distinct sector mix compared to value-oriented peers. This leads to overweight positions of 16.1% and 8.6% relative to the group. On the flip side, the fund is underweight in virtually all sectors, particularly industrials (-8.8%) and energy (-6.2%), areas in which peers rely on these segments to bring value to investors.

As a result, SEIV has a moderate sector correlation with most funds in the 0.48 to 0.62 range. The exception is IUSV, which has a relatively high correlation of 0.88 with SEIV, as both share a large exposure to technology and maintain a modest stance in industrials.

Attractive Valuation

At an 11.1 P/E, SEIV trades at a steep 50% discount to the Russell 1000’s 22.4. This, of course, is consistent with the fund’s goal of holding an undervalued portfolio. The valuation gap is apparent in a sector-by-sector comparison with the benchmark, as all sectors trade at a discount. This makes the fund’s significant position in technology, a high-multiple sector by nature, not a detractor to its overall value profile, as some of the fund’s core holdings in the sector, like Hewlett Packard Enterprise, Dell Technologies, Micron, and Qualcomm, trade at particularly attractive valuations, all below 17x P/E.

But the fund lags in growth, especially given its historical earnings growth of 3.7% which is nearly half of the Russell 1000’s 7.4%, driven by the fund’s defensive profile in segments like technology, healthcare, and financial services, as highlighted by holdings in Micron (3Y EPS growth 1.1%) and AIG (-5.3%), for instance.

With an EBITDA margin of 25.7% and an ROE of 46.9%, profitability measures are lower than the benchmark by nearly 10 percentage points. Weaker margins are spread across most sectors, including technology, financial services, the biggest detractors, as well as healthcare, industrials, and consumer-related sectors, since many companies trading at a discount also exhibit lower margins than core holdings of the broader equity indexes, such as big techs or names like Visa, Mastercard, Eli Lilly, and AbbVie.

SEIV trades at the low end of the trading range compared to peers, along with VFVA (10.5x P/E) and WTV (12.2x). Valuations of these two funds seem justified, in my view, given their modest stance in technology and a large allocation to smaller companies: VFVA (55%) and WTV (32%), which usually trade at a discount to the rest of the market. On the flip side, P/E multiples of IWD, IUSV, and AIVL sit above 16%, still in the value camp, but at a significant premium to other peers, primarily due to their large exposure to mid- and large-cap companies relative. In summary, despite a heavy allocation to technology, SEIV still looks cheap, driven by a value approach across all sectors, including technology, and a relatively diversified allocation to all market sizes, making it one of those so-called deep-value funds in the category.

Outperforming Peers

With inception on May 18, 2022, SEIV has recently completed three years of activity. During this period, its annual return of 22.3% slightly trailed the Russell 1000’s 23.2%, as broader equity indexes have benefited from the performance of big techs, particularly in 2023 and 2024. Still, SEIV’s performance has been quite strong compared to other value funds, with the highest return in the 3-year period, and it has consistently stayed within the top 3 funds in each of the three years.

SEIV’s volatility has been marginally above the Russell 1000, with a 3-year beta of 1.03 and capture ratios indicating a modest tendency to amplify downside market moves. To be sure, that is not the norm among value funds, with four out of six funds exhibiting more contained volatility than the benchmark. VFVA and WTV also show above-average volatility, due to their exposure to small caps, which are typically more volatile than their large-cap counterparts. In summary, while most value peers usually see more moderate price action during declines, the trade-off is that SEIV has outperformed in rallies, as these more conservative peers have shown upside capture ratios lower than 100, which helps explain SEIV’s outperformance during a period that has been predominantly bullish for stocks.

On trading activity, SEIV’s daily average volume of $4.33 million is well below large funds like IWD and IUSV, but remains ahead of VFVA and AIVL. However, its bid-ask spread (0.16%) is the second widest, which is a disadvantage for short-term trades, especially if compared to most liquid funds with spreads below 0.04%. The fund’s turnover of 32% is below the peer group average, but still higher than IWD and DFUV, while its expense ratio (0.15%) is in line with peers, which I would say looks reasonable for an actively managed fund. It also offers a dividend yield of 1.53%, above the broader market average of about 1.10%, but slightly lower than peers, while its tracking error in the 5% to 6% range is the lowest in the group. Finally, the fund’s 3-year Sharpe ratio of 1.20 is by far the highest among peers, reflecting its outperformance despite a higher volatility.

Final Thoughts

SEIV’s structure reflects an attempt to capture undervalued opportunities across the market capitalization spectrum. And its tilt toward technology, financials, and communication services sets it apart from value peers, typically more concentrated in defensive sectors. However, it has also contributed to the fund’s higher volatility. Despite the sector mix, SEIV trades at roughly half of the Russell 1000’s valuation, leaving it in deep-value territory. The flip side is moderate earnings growth that may limit the upside going forward.

In sum, my view is that SEIV’s attractive valuation and diversified sector mix look well-suited for a market that seems constructive overall, but shows mixed economic signals, while trade tensions continue to affect short-term sentiment. On top of that, the fund’s sector allocation makes it an option for investors targeting value, but seeking to diversify away from conventional sector allocations tilted to defensive sectors like healthcare, consumer staples, and energy.

data.title: Home Buyers' Strike Drags On Relentlessly, Despite Lower Mortgage Rates
data.description:
So mortgage rates are not the problem; they’re at the low end of the pre-QE historical range. Those exploded prices are.

Despite slightly lower mortgage rates, demand for homes by homebuyers who need a mortgage dropped further in the latest reporting week, the third weekly decline of purchase mortgage applications in a row, according to data from the Mortgage Bankers Association on Wednesday. Compared to the same week in 2019, purchase mortgage applications were down by 34%.

Demand in the housing market plunged three years ago, after home prices had exploded over the preceding two-year period of a mega-FOMO-driven frenzy, fueled by mortgage rates the Fed had recklessly repressed via QE to way below the rate of inflation. So mortgage rates are not the problem; those exploded prices are.

These applications for mortgages to purchase a home are a measure of demand that may become actual home sales in the future and are therefore an indicator of future home sales. And they remain dismal.

The average weekly mortgage rate for conforming 30-year fixed mortgages declined a hair to 6.42%, according to the MBA on Wednesday. This measure bottomed out last year in September and early October 2024 at 6.13%, just before the Fed started cutting interest rates.

Last year, from mid-September through mid-December, the Fed cut its policy rates three times, by a total of 100 basis points, despite re-accelerating inflation. The bond market, including the mortgage market, got spooked about a lax Fed in light of re-accelerating inflation and went the opposite way. From mid-September 2024 through early January 2025, mortgage rates jumped by 100 basis points.

After that episode, the Fed talked hawkish and put rate cuts on hold to keep a lid on the bond market. Even now, as rate cuts have started again, the Fed is talking about “careful” rate cuts and being “concerned” about the “rising inflation” and “watching it closely,” etc., etc., while reacting to labor-market weakness. The last thing they want is another spike in long-term yields.

The Fed’s reckless policy of pushing mortgage rates far below the rate of inflation via QE, including the purchase of mortgage-backed securities, pushed mortgage rates below 3% even as inflation was raging toward 9%, thereby creating the most negative “real” (inflation-adjusted) mortgage rates ever.

For homebuyers, the steeply negative real mortgage rates were better than free money, and when money is free, prices don’t matter, and homebuyers went nuts. During this period I called it the Most Reckless Fed Ever. And as we now know, its recklessness has done enormous long-lasting damage to the housing market.

A funny thing happened with refis. That small drop in mortgage rates in mid-September by just a few basis points apparently pierced a magic line, and a bunch of homeowners suddenly refinanced mortgages, causing a spike in refi applications, but it lasted only for two weeks, and then it was over again.

Refi applications edged down further in the latest reporting week, after having plunged over the prior two weeks from the two-week-wonder spike.

It seems a bunch of homeowners had been sitting on the edge of their chairs waiting for rates to inch down to a magic level, and then they pounced, and there were no more people left sitting on the edge of their chairs? It’s funny because mortgage rates barely moved over those six weeks.

The previous sudden spike in demand for refi mortgages occurred a year ago in September 2024, when the MBA’s measure of the average weekly 30-year fixed mortgage rate dropped as low as 6.13%, before surging again:

Refi mortgage applications (red in the chart below) and mortgage interest rates (blue) are in an inverse relationship. The below-3% mortgage rates while inflation was raging toward 9% caused not only an explosion of home prices but also a historic refinance boom when a huge number of mortgages were refinanced.

That spike of refi mortgage applications a few weeks ago was small compared to the boom years of negative real mortgage rates, but exceeded the volume in 2017, 2018, and early 2019.

This refi boom in 2020-2021 caused a large portion of mortgages to carry very low interest rates, and homeowners now cling to these low-interest-rate mortgages as if they were valuable assets. Nevertheless, life happens, and homeowners are gradually getting out of these mortgages and loosening the phenomenon of the “lock-in effect.”

The share of below-3% mortgages outstanding declined in Q2 to 20.4% of all mortgages outstanding, the smallest share since Q2 2021 (red in the chart below). And the share of 3%-3.99% mortgages declined to 32.1%, the smallest share since Q3 2019 (blue).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nestle: Strategy Update Is A Sign Of Change (Upgrade)
data.description:
After five years that the stock has been essentially dead money with very little growth, declining margins, and two CEO transitions, Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) seems to finally turn a new leaf.

The newly elected CEO, Philipp Navratil, seems to be actually willing to do what it takes to inject energy into sleepy old Nestlé, with no better evidence than his willingness to say the 'L' word, for layoffs.

With Nestlé set to cut nearly 6% of its workforce, I argue this is the sign of change investors have waited for, and upgrade Nestlé to a 'Buy'.

Sometimes, It's All About Chance, As Philipp Navratil Is The Accidental CEO Nestlé Needed

From 1981 to 2024, Nestlé had four CEOs, with an average term of over 10 years. Since 2024, Nestlé already replaced its CEO twice.

Phillipp Navratil, the current CEO, was with the company for a long time and last served as the CEO of the Nespresso division. He was promoted to the CEO role after the former CEO, Laurent Freixe, was dismissed after he was found to have a romantic relationship with an employee.

Personal issues aside, I argue that this is a great development for Nestlé shareholders. I had a lot of criticism for Freixe's plans, which I viewed as lacking teeth, or as I called it, a "Let’s Do Good Things & Stop Doing Bad Things” plan.

In my last article, I wrote that:

If they were really ambitious with cost savings, we would have seen significant layoffs by now.

Well, it's finally happening.

New CEO's Plan Signals Real Transformation

One month into his role, the presentation from Phillipp Navratil tells me that this guy has been thinking about how he would fix Nestlé for quite some time. His plan includes four pillars:

The first two pillars are things the previous CEO talked about as well, and they are turnaround 101 in large-scale consumer companies. In short, they want to focus on growing products that meet consumer demand where it is, aggressively pursue growth areas, and ruthlessly cut underperforming lines.

This is easy to say, hard to do, and it requires a certain personality. With the previous CEO, I was more skeptical, but the new CEO gives reason to be optimistic, because of pillars 3 and 4.

A company like Nestlé, that's been around for a long time and has 277,000 employees, can get complacent. Many employees are old timers; there's a lot of bureaucracy, and too many people who give their input.

In situations like these, individual performance tends to be left behind, while seniority gets prioritized. Well, the new CEO is saying, not under his watch. He wants to create a performance culture, with performance-based compensation and clear KPIs.

Lastly, the cherry on top, the new CEO is showing he means business, by targeting a 6% workforce reduction, laying off 16,000 employees. He also plans to create a more agile organization, which should lead to faster decision-making, more efficient operations, and accountability.

Nestlé's Results Are Also Showing Signs Of Improvement

Nestlé only discloses sales figures on a quarterly basis, whereas full financials are reported on a semiannual basis. The sales figures were quite strong in Q3 and marked a meaningful improvement.

Real internal growth, which is a metric that measures mix and volume, was up 1.5% in the quarter, the highest level in quite some time. Meanwhile, pricing was up 2.8%, leading to a total organic growth of 4.3%, a post-pandemic record.

All zones had positive RIGs and positive organic growth, while all product segments except for Confectionery had positive RIG, and all of them had positive organic growth.

Nestlé also shared the big difference between its winning and losing SKUs, whereby the priority growth products have grown 14% in the first nine months of 2025, while the underperforming lines were flat.

This goes to emphasize the opportunity that a cold-hearted decision maker can capitalize on if they execute properly.

Looking ahead, they have maintained the medium-term guidance for now, but increased their cost savings plan to CHF 3.0 billion, up from CHF 2.5 billion. As they progress, I believe more aggressive targets could be delivered.

Valuation

In previous bearish articles, I argued that Nestlé is facing structural issues that are hard to overcome. I wrote:

Simply put, companies that don't grow revenues and earnings have very few avenues to keep up with the market. And companies that trade at relatively high valuations ahead of a three-year stagnation are very likely to not even keep up with bonds.

That is, in a nutshell, the story of Nestlé of the past three years and why I remain bearish, even after the appointment of a new CEO.

I just think that the company's issues are structural, and unless a very aggressive CEO comes in and takes almost a private-equity-like approach to a turnaround, I continue to believe the stock will provide poor returns.

Well, at CHF 82 per share, Nestlé trades below 19 times forward earnings. Translating to ADR, shares will probably open around $102, which would reflect 18 times forward earnings and 17 times next year's estimates.

This is no longer a high valuation. In fact, it's a low point for Nestlé historically, and it's about in line with other consumer staple peers like PepsiCo (PEP) and Unilever (UL).

Meanwhile, Nestlé is now an arrow pointing up story. Growth is accelerating, and margins are likely to improve under the new CEO.

As such, I think they could recover to at least a 20x multiple, reflecting a target price of $115 in the near term.

Conclusion

There was never a question about Nestlé's wide moat and strong brands. The concern was a lack of growth and an unwillingness to do what's needed to reignite the company's earnings growth story.

I believe the new CEO has the right plan, and my impression is that he's the man for the job. As numbers already begin to improve, I upgrade Nestlé to 'Buy'.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Enel Chile: Poised To Benefit From De-Risked Regulations Amid Renewable Expansion
data.description:
About a year ago, I described Enel Chile's (NYSE:ENIC) investment thesis as follows:

[Enel Chile] has been in the final stages of integrating its renewable energy plan. This involves expanding its portfolio, maintaining its balance sheet at optimal levels, and gradually reducing capital expenditures (CapEx). Although this involves being more frugal to reduce costs and keep operations running smoothly, Enel Chile has provided guidance promising to increase the progressive payout to more than 50% over the next two years, making the company well-positioned to be a reliable income stock for a dividend-focused portfolio.

Since then, the ADR of the company based in Santiago, Chile, has performed quite well, returning around 43% counting dividends and outperforming the S&P 500.

I would list the main progress as (1) regulatory adjustments and tariff stability, bringing greater financial predictability to the company; (2) increased renewable generation and the implementation of energy storage, bringing good prospects for new sources of revenue from 2026 onwards; and (3) improved EBITDA and cash generation being the result of the effectiveness of the company's investment strategies.

Looking ahead, Enel Chile is preparing to report 3Q25 results at the end of October, and the outlook for 2H25 is more favorable, especially on the hydro issue, which turned out to be a relevant headwind in 1H25 (albeit partially mitigated by a diverse portfolio), with the company well positioned to at least report numbers in line with its lower end of the annual guidance. I also see good progress in terms of regulation, in a much more favorable scenario than a year ago, which could bring better predictability of future cash generation and keep dividends at attractive levels.

Over the course of this article, I will update my thesis and detail the reasons why I remain constructive about Enel Chile, especially with a view to the long term.

Chile’s Net-Zero Agenda and Enel Chile’s Strategic Adaptation

Chile continues to be a leader in terms of the energy transition towards electrification and decarbonization. The country has a net zero agenda that aims for (1) 80% renewable energy by 2030; (2) 0% coal power plants by 2040; and (3) 100% new urban transport additions, in addition to the sale of light and medium vehicles to be zero emission by 2035.

Since 2019, Enel Chile has had to adapt both operationally and strategically to align with Chile's net-zero agenda. Within the movements, we can simplistically list three as the most relevant: (1) the increase in renewable energies - considering that today 78% of the net installed capacity is from renewable matrices; (2) investing in storage technologies (BESS), which basically consist of battery systems to store more affordable energy during off-peak hours and sell it when there is high demand - or when the price is high; and (3) decarbonizing and modernizing their plants, from closing coal plants and securing gas contracts as a backup solution until renewable solutions prove efficient to the full extent.

Of course, this process is complex, and it comes with a challenging regulatory framework that has brought a handful of setbacks. For example, there was an Energy Stabilization Mechanism (a.k.a. PEC 1.0) that came into being to protect consumers from very high tariff increases through government price freezes.

The result was that energy companies didn't get paid everything, which generated large volumes of accounts receivable. In an attempt to maintain fair tariffs for consumers without jeopardizing the financial viability of distributors, PEC 2.0 and 3.0 were created, which changed billing rules and subsidies, and this only increased regulatory uncertainty.

Other regulatory headwinds were the return allowed for distributors falling from 8.5% to 6% post-tax, reducing the attractiveness of investments, and a VAD (value added of distribution) for 2020-2024, only published in 2024, delaying and leaving utilities in Chile without knowing exactly how much they can charge, disrupting planning.

In the meantime, hydrological setbacks, given that hydroelectric plants depend on rainfall, have created a risk of energy shortages and increased costs for more expensive thermal generation, exacerbating the challenge of planning a resilient energy matrix in the face of climate change (which BESS is seeking).

However, especially in the course of this year, things finally seem to be moving. Regulated tariffs have been updated, without the accumulation of PECs and with PECs 2.0 and 3.0 being implemented; the 2020-2024 VAD has been published and is in force, and the 2024-2028 VAD is underway; the regulation of BESS capacity is a new regulatory framework, among other things.

In short, if many regulatory issues caused uncertainty between 2020 and 2024, I would say, it seems that in 2025, only a few cause uncertainty.

Enel Chile in Transition

To better situate Enel Chile's position in the midst of the energy transition, it is worth analyzing the Load Factor (LF) metric, i.e., basically how much energy a plant actually generates compared to what it could generate if it were on all the time. When there is a high LF, it means that the plant is being used a lot (generating more revenue). If it has a low LF, it is underused, or there is excess capacity in the system, or even a bottleneck.

From the data on net installed capacity and net production in 1H25, CCGT thermal (45%) and hydroelectric (34%) have the highest LF, with solar/wind/geothermal at 20% and oil/gas at 23%. BESS still has low penetration in a dormant situation.

This shows that thermal and hydro plants are, in fact, Enel Chile's main sources of cash generation, ensuring supply when and while renewables do not produce enough.

Renewable plants show, on the one hand, that there is a very large installed capacity, but that it has not yet been possible to translate this into widespread use, which leads to pressure on margins and reveals inefficiencies and market bottlenecks.

On the other hand, this also shows where the current opportunity lies for Enel Chile: whoever manages to store or use this energy better, for example, with batteries (BESS) or smart contracts, can capture extra value.

When we look at the total volume generated versus the volume distributed/spot, it is visible that in 1H25, own production fell, but the energy sold (total portfolio) was practically stable between 1H25 and 1H25. This means a greater dependence on third-party purchases and the spot market, which tends to lead to a lower unit margin.

As for spot performance, which ended up at 2.3 TWh in 1H25-very close to the 2.5 TWh in 1H24-it hasn't been very relevant, but it also remains important for covering any hydro plant gaps given the seasonal weather that has been recurring for Enel Chile. Regarding these hydrological issues, I would say that the ability to dispatch thermal and the diversified portfolio are very valuable for reducing systemic risks, which I think is slightly positive in the medium term.

In terms of financial performance, we can see that the consequences of these lower unit margins are reflected in net income falling 8% to $246 million. But to give you an idea of how relevant regulatory issues are in the thesis, Enel Chile's Funds From Operations (FFO) (or Adjusted Operating Cash Flow) in 1H25 rose 7.8x versus 1H24, a result marked by delayed receivables and the PEC.

Within the current operating circumstances of Enel Chile's portfolio, 1H25 CapEx, amounting to $157 million, was allocated 40% to grids (transmission and distribution infrastructure), 31% to thermal, and 29% to renewables plus BESS. The projection in the company's strategic plan for 2025E is to distribute a total of $0.8 billion in CapEx, half of which will be dedicated to BESS. For 2026 and 2027, the projection is half a billion in CapEx per year, with a heavier investment in renewables of $0.3 billion in 2027E.

3Q25 and 2H25 Expectations for Enel Chile

Enel Chile is preparing to report 3Q25 earnings at the end of October. For 2H25, Enel Chile should have a more stable EBITDA and cash flow, slightly higher generation from the Los Cóndores (hydro) project. This project started connection in 4Q24, and with an expected COD (commercial operating date) in March 2025, it should already contribute to 2H25, adding around 350 GWh of annual generation—which supports a small increase in operating revenue and cash support. On the other hand, the three hybrid BESS projects (of around 450 MW) will only enter commercial operation in 1H26, so the impact on EBITDA should not yet appear in 2H25.

Regarding hydro, Enel Chile's target remains at 10.7 TWh in 2025, based on the 10-year average, even though 2H25 tends to be sensitive to hydro variability.

However, historically, hydro tends to have higher production in the second half of the year as normal rainfall occurs. Enel Chile has also secured contracts with gas suppliers to ensure that thermal generation (CCGT) can meet demand even in periods of hydro volatility. Therefore, I would expect lower operational risk and greater EBITDA margin protection.

Regarding the regulatory framework, Enel Chile has already received a large part of the PEC credits via factoring ($630 million plus $250 million in 2Q25), and will not accumulate new credits because the tariffs have been updated. This gives more predictability to operating cash and free flow, especially in H2 2025.

That said, I would estimate FFO between $280-320M in 3Q25 and $300-350M in 4Q25, incorporating monetized PEC and no new accruals. Remember that, based on the targets that Enel Chile reported in its strategic plan last year, the EBITDA and net income projections for 2025 are already halfway to achieving the lower end of the guidance.

Considering the company's historical payout, which recently distributed more than 100% of net profits, it is plausible that the payout will be between 50-70% of FFO.

I would therefore assign a possible distribution of between $500 and $700 million, which, at current prices, would imply a yield of between 3.4% and 4.7% for 2025—slightly less optimistic than the projection of the only analyst covering ENIC on Seeking Alpha's dividend estimates panel.

Valuation, Risks, and Final Remarks

Based on the company's own 2025 EBITDA target of $1.0-1.1 billion, this would put the company at a forward multiple of ~8x, significantly below the industry average of ~12x, but slightly above the company's historical average of 7.3x.

One must consider that this is a cheap multiple for a utility with predictable cash flow and relatively stable dividends ranging between (~3.4-4.7%).

However, the discount versus international peers is comprehensible considering the high regulatory risks that the thesis has within the Chilean energy market, which is even the most highlighted point in the risks section of the company's filings.

Another risk, but one that could potentially become a reward, is that we are on the verge of presidential elections in Chile, which will take place in mid-November. The incumbent president, Gabriel Boric (pro-state policies and left wing) will not be able to stand for re-election, and the name that has been emerging in the polls is that of Evelyn Matthei (center-right wing), who would be more pleasing to the market given expectations of friendlier private sector policies and a possible regulatory stability that would be welcome for Enel Chile's thesis.

In operational terms, the hydro risk remains the most considerable for 2H25 margins to materialize and deliver results within the company's guidance—although there is good portfolio diversification to mitigate these effects, and 2H25 usually has a higher rainfall volume than 1H25.

Finally, I would say that Enel Chile remains a cheap utility, still yielding attractive dividends and well organized to benefit from its renewable portfolio, and especially new sources of revenue from BESS.

I believe that the pre-3Q25 and 2H25 scenario is constructive, especially in the current multiples, with evident regulatory advances, and also based on its resilience and the operational efficiency of its diversified portfolio.

data.title: Amazon's Stalemate: Earning Income While Waiting For The Next Catalyst
data.description:
Since I last reviewed Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) (TSX:AMZN:CA) and downgraded it to a Hold (after reaping in ~31% profits), it has underperformed the S&P 500 by 9 percentage points. Since July, the S&P 500 has rallied by ~6%, while Amazon is down by 3% from the time I wrote. Not a huge underperformance, but it feels good to get the timing right so perfectly.

I generally believe that Amazon is a core portfolio allocation, and such stocks should not be judged for a few percentage points here or there in the short term. The reason why I downgraded Amazon to a Hold in July was part profit booking (having timed a decent entry in April) and part lack of any near term catalyst visibility.

Has Anything Changed?

Overall, the reasons for the downgrade were primarily a lack of momentum that Amazon often suffers from in phases. Since July, lack of any near term catalyst has rendered an even more benign profile to the stock. The valuations have hardly moved from the July levels of ~14x in EV to forward EBITDA ratio terms. That is not expensive, but not the usual value territory (around ~11-12x) where Amazon Buys have been more rewarded in the recent past.

The macro environment has changed though - there has been a 25 bps rate cut in the interim that fuels hopes of supportive equity valuations - particularly high tech valuations. Although valuations may be breathing a bit easier, there is enough left in terms of macro pressures to keep the broader market pressure factor roughly intact from where we were in July. Tariff concerns, that were starting to work through negotiations and ceasefire signals, have again landed in uncertain territory. Consumer spending pressures are still very much a threat as well.

Several companies have reported decent numbers in Q2, a quarter where tariff impacts should have started to show up - easing concerns around the extent of impact of tariffs. The tariff concerns seem to be absorbed a lot better today (barring the new China developments). However, uncertainties are still a drag and supply chain disruptions are concerns even if the quantum of financial impact looks slightly better than initially expected. You can read a simplistic tariff model and its expected range of impact on Arista here. The impact is certainly not a difference between a company turning financially fragile from profit making - but a dent on supply chain and geopolitical positioning is a drag, at least for an e-commerce player like Amazon. So overall, while the macro environment has changed, an optimal macro environment required to support a Buy thesis has not emerged.

On the business front, AWS grew by 17.5% in Q2 2025, a slight improvement over the 17% growth seen in Q1, but still short of my 20-25% growth expectations that becomes a rerating trigger. The advertising growth of 19% in Q2 is a continuation of strong momentum in an expanding segment beyond AWS - I expect advertising to remain strong going into H2 2025 and the foreseeable future. Advertising is a silver lining and a decent supporting factor, but not thesis altering on its own. The core thesis-altering factor in my view is returning growth in AWS and that is yet to be seen.

AWS should keep growing at levels healthy enough to allay concerns of competitive pressures from Azure and Google Cloud. Then there are other catalysts that have not been on fire and belied market expectations of a growth catalyst in the near term. Amazon's healthcare unit is yet to establish itself as a growing and profitable unit despite years of investments, including acquisitions. Kuiper prospects have also been dampened due to multiple delays pushing back commercial monetization to not before 2026.

Playing the Stalemate

There have been very few positive catalysts in adjacent businesses. AWS is waiting for a rebound, and international profitability remains elusive for the e-commerce segment. While the story is clearly bland now and the wait for a material catalyst continues, everything is not negative. There have been developments that support Amazon from further material downsides. AWS is still not in concern territory, and advertising growth continues to impress, for example.

Amazon is in a zone where I think it will continue to trade between $200-250 till a catalyst emerges. Amazon has a habit of oscillating between psychological round numbers before breaking into the next range when catalysts emerge or momentum starts picking up. This stalemate presents a perfect opportunity to implement a "wheel-like" strategy. A strategy of setting up monthly put sell positions at the support point of $200 (or higher for aggressive investors) - earning premiums if Amazon continues to trade above the $200 mark. Any dip below $200 presents a discounted entry point for investors who have a bullish long term thesis on Amazon (which I do).

Amazon's long term thesis is sufficiently supported just by the scale of its e-commerce presence, access to data that can be leveraged across its adjacent businesses (targeted ads, healthcare suggestions, etc.) and a flywheel effect due to its wide ecosystem. Add to that the fact that valuations are still conducive - Amazon has had the least valuation explosion among other tech names in the Magnificent Seven pack and even within the defensive tech pack (like MSFT and GOOG). Historically, pullbacks are the best times to enter Amazon, particularly beyond nearest support round numbers - $200 in this case.

So assignment at strikes of $200 is a discounted entry in the wheel strategy, I suggest - long term investors will find those entry points value supportive. In fact the reason I suggest the wheel strategy for Amazon is because I don't mind owning it at $200 at all.

The $200 put option is now trading at ~$4.4 for a mid-November expiry (a month away). That is 2.2% premium earned in just a month (~26% annualized). The premium collected could go up if Amazon nears $200 and go down to up to ~0.5%, if Amazon trades at $240-250. Still the average income from such a strategy is lucrative enough and can be dynamically adjusted to generate returns anywhere between 5-30% within a year. The worst outcome in this trade is an assignment at $200, which is a great entry in my view.

The income potential is not only higher than Amazon's ~15% gains in the past one year, but the most appropriate positioning given the fundamental and technical drags in the stock. The other risk of this strategy is Amazon's breakaway from the trading range of $200-250, leading to an opportunity loss. That is a lesser concern as of today, as investors can ideally switch to a Buy Amazon position from this strategy, whenever catalysts or momentum emerge. There is none at the moment.

Another way to play Amazon's stalemate is using the YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (AMZY). This reduces drawdown risks (again gives a discounted entry point indirectly if Amazon falls below $200). However, AMZY pays out capital aggressively and needs reinvestments to stay fully invested. Fundamentally, AMZY works on a call writing principle - in contrast to my cash-secured put-selling suggestion. There are nuances between the two routes, but both could work for Amazon's strict trading range.

Summarizing, Amazon is a core portfolio holding at the right prices. Today, we are very near those price levels, but a lack of catalysts and momentum justifies an income oriented approach over a full conviction Buy for conservative traders. As catalysts emerge, investors may switch over to a full fledged Buy, or earn decent income through the option enabled strategies I have mentioned. For now, I keep Amazon a Hold and ride this period with an income outlook of 5-30%, even if the range is not broken or the lack of catalysts persist.

Risks to the Thesis

The only risk to the thesis is if Amazon breaks out of the $200-250 trading range. On the downside, there is no risk apart from a buying opportunity at $200 or slightly below (given the premiums earned). Of course, AWS headwinds or any extreme loss of leadership position in its e-commerce moat could weaken Amazon's Buy at $200 thesis too. But that looks like a far-fetched case to plan for, looking at today's dynamics. We are talking about situations like a low double digit AWS growth or flat e-commerce sales for a couple of quarters. Even in those cases, a Buy below $200 is better risk adjusted than now.

The upside opportunity loss is a more real risk and very likely given Amazon's relatively subdued performance in the past few years. Any advertising, adjacent business growth, AWS rebound or value unlocking overall due to the Amazon flywheel could easily push Amazon to the next trading range of $250-$300. In this case too, investors following a wheel strategy can still end up with 5-10% income for the year, but lose out 20-30% upside opportunity. Of course, I have maintained that investors should switch to a traditional long position when catalysts appear - there are no such signs as of now.

Earnings Preview

Amazon's recent earnings history has been positive surprises for the past few quarters and I do not see any change to that trend in Q3 2025. However, I feel the beat will be subdued given expectations on holiday season ramp up, continued enterprise AI spending will be balanced by uncertain tariff impacts and consumer demand weakening. How much the Prime Day and promotions affect the e-commerce number will be interesting to see. If e-commerce sales decelerate to anywhere below 10% in North America, I would consider that a negative given the Prime Day quarter expectations. I am looking at a non GAAP EPS slightly higher than expectations at $1.6 and revenue to clock around $180b (supported by mostly positive FX impacts from international sales).

Beyond headline EPS and revenue numbers, I will keep a keen watch on AWS growth - betting on continued growth from 17% YoY in Q1 to 17.5% in Q2 to 18% now. Any miss there could weaken sentiment on AWS. I am also building in an expectation for growth above 20% in advertising - so any miss there will also likely dampen sentiment.

Guidance and updates on Project Kuiper and capex spending in general will be important. Any management commentary on tariff negotiations and progress, particularly with a new round of uncertainties on China tariffs, will be important to align expectations going ahead.

Any AWS growth of 20%+ could lead to potential rally and affect the thesis - investors should switch from the wheel strategy to an outright Buy position - as this will be a solid rerating catalyst. I don't see the odds of such positive catalysts as very high. On the flip side, any disappointment may push Amazon below $200 and open up fresh long-term entries (per the thesis).

data.title: Bank OZK: Regional Bank Fears Create Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) plunged 7% on Thursday as the regional bank sector was slammed amid fears of increasing credit losses. Firms like Jefferies (JEF), Fifth Third (FITB), Western Alliance (WAL), and Zions (ZION) have all flagged losses, creating genuine worry about private credit. While OZK has a much different loan book focused in commercial real estate ("CRE"), that did not help shares. After the bell, the company then reported mixed results, which may not benefit sentiment. I last covered shares in July, rating the stock a "strong buy," but performance has been disappointing, with OZK losing 10%. Given updated financials and new macro worries, now is a good time to revisit shares. I remain bullish.

In the company's third quarter, Bank OZK earned $1.59, which was $0.10 below consensus, even as revenue rose by 6% to $450 million. EPS was up 1% sequentially and about 3% from a year ago, reaching a new record. Some of this miss was driven by a smaller asset base, given elevated repayments, which I view as a net positive given lingering credit concerns. Additionally, provisions for losses ticked up $13 million to $48 million as OZK built reserves. Higher charge-offs were not the driver of the reserve build. This increase was about a $0.10 detriment to sequential EPS, accounting for most of the miss. Provision levels do remain relatively low.

Strong deposit activity supports NIM

Net interest income rose $34 million from a year ago to $414 million thanks to aggressive asset growth. This was also up $17 million sequentially. I expect most of this gain to be reversed in Q4 as the impact of Fed rate cuts filters through, likely resulting in about $400 million of NII. I would note that management cited a "goal" of growing NII next year, but I view a "goal" as less confident than actual guidance. Its net interest margin ("NIM") fell 1 bp sequentially, and it is 20 bps lower over the past year. I expect a similar amount of NIM compression over the next year, given my expectation for 3-4 Fed rate cuts.

Turning to its balance sheet, OZK ended the quarter with $34 billion in deposits, up $500 million in Q3 and $3.5 billion from a year ago. OZK has been an excellent accumulator of deposits, aided by above-average interest rates. This strong deposit growth has helped to fuel its excellent loan growth while ensuring the company maintains adequate liquidity. While the absolute cost of its deposits is relatively high, they are down 67 bps from a year ago, an excellent pull-through of Fed rate cuts. I expect deposit costs to fall in Q4 given Fed rate cuts, but given CDs, deposit rates will likely take a bit more time to react than loan yields.

CRE repayments surged in Q3

Bank OZK had $32.9 billion in loans, up nearly $3 billion this year. However, shockingly for a bank that has grown loans rapidly, they actually declined by $0.2 billion in the quarter. That is because the bank received a record $2.4 billion in real estate principal payments vs just $0.7 billion in originations (the slowest in over five years). In past quarters, management had said that payments would accelerate materially in H2, but this exceeded my expectations. Capital market access for CRE borrowers improved meaningfully over the past ~4-5 months, and that is now hitting OZK's results, after low prepayments exaggerated loan growth in H1.

I view these payments as a clear positive. There has been ongoing concern about the CRE-heavy nature of OZK's loan book, and it has been a very slow process diversifying. These payments help to accelerate that effort. Additionally, payments of this magnitude go a long way to showcasing OZK's strong underwriting and the fact that not all CRE exposure is bad. This development adds to my comfort in OZK's credit quality as well as my view that the valuation overhang from it is not entirely justified. Nearly, all of this portfolio was underwritten post-COVID, consistent with the company's strong track record of repayment.

As you can see below, OZK has about 58% of its loan book in real estate. This is still extremely high with most of the sector below 35%. However, it actually has been falling considerably. Due to these high Q2 repayments, the real estate share fell by 230 bps sequentially, and it is 10% below its all-time high. I expect real estate exposure to fall below 50% by late next year. Given OZK's specialized underwriting skills in real estate, I would expect the share to stabilize close to 50%.

Credit quality is solid

While commercial real estate was once seen as a liability, this exposure actually may make OZK appear comparatively safe. With the recent spate of high-profile corporate defaults including Tricolor and First Brands, suddenly, business lending appears riskier. This would especially be the case if the economy rolls over into recession. Declining interest rates also helps to support CRE valuations, making it easier to refinance. Some CRE asset classes, like office and select multifamily, have clear challenges, but if private credit is really the next epicenter of losses, OZK may be fairly well positioned compared to peers, a reversal of the more recent perception.

The company has $150 million in non-accrual loans and $79 million in foreclosure, so reserves are about 3x this, a healthy level. Importantly, on its real estate portfolio, the loan-to-value is just 46% on average, greatly insulating the company from potential losses. This is a key reason I am comfortable with OZK's CRE exposure. It now has $680 million in reserves, with reserves up $14 million sequentially. As you can see below, OZK has been steadily building reserves while also reporting record profits. Net charge-offs were a muted 41bps.

Capital is strong, enabling shareholder returns

I would also note that unlike other banks that have been saddled with large losses on their investment securities portfolio, OZK has a relatively small portfolio of just $2.8 billion. The portfolio also yields 4.25%, up from 3.00% a year ago as low-yielding securities matured. I do not expect much further yield appreciation, and I expect the portfolio to remain in the $2.5-$3.0 billion range depending on deposit and loan growth.

Importantly, Bank OZK has an excellent capital position with an 11.55% tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio. This capital position provides plenty of flexibility for ongoing asset growth, not to mention ensuring the security of its capital returns to investors, while also providing a cushion in case loan losses do come in above expectations.

In its commentary, management suggested that buybacks could accelerate under its $200 million buyback program. Its share count is down only 0.7% over the past year, speaking to how little repurchase activity there has been to this point. Additionally, OZK has increased its dividend for 61 straight quarters, and shares now have a 3.6% dividend yield.

Shares are too cheap to ignore

Back in July, I was targeting $6.25-$6.50 in EPS this year. With these results, I see EPS coming in at the low-end of my range, or about $6.25, due to slightly higher reserves and lower loan balances, due to strong repayment activity. OZK has a $45 tangible book value, and I believe this can help put a floor under shares, making now an attractive entry point. I previously thought OZK could trade at 10x earnings, still below many peers.

With increased worry about credit in general, I see multiple compression pressures across the regional banking complex. Still, OZK is below 8x, which I view as extremely cheap, especially as its loan book is not centered at all in private credit or business lending. As such, I still view OZK as a top pick in the sector and reiterate my strong buy. Even at 9x, shares can trade into the upper-$50s, and some incremental buybacks can provide support. This regional bank scare is creating opportunities for value investors, and Bank OZK should be bought.

data.title: Fed Chair Powell: Balance Sheet Reduction May End Soon
data.description:
By Mike Maharrey

In another sign that we are entering an era of even looser monetary policy, Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell hinted that balance sheet reduction is about to come to an end.

“Our long-stated plan is to stop balance sheet runoff when reserves are somewhat above the level we judge consistent with ample reserve conditions. We may approach that point in coming months, and we are closely monitoring a wide range of indicators to inform this decision.”

In practice, this means the central bank will stop reducing its holdings of Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities, maintaining the size of its balance sheet at the current level.

Balance sheet reduction, or quantitative tightening (QT), pulls liquidity out of the financial system by reducing bank reserves and shifting government debt financing to the private sector. This tightens funding conditions and market debt. It is effectively deflationary.

Powell said, “Some signs have begun to emerge that liquidity conditions are gradually tightening,” thereby necessitating the end of QT.

More significantly, an end balance sheet reduction would take some pressure off the Treasury market, as the central bank would have to purchase new bonds as the old ones mature. In effect, this would create additional artificial demand in the Treasury market.

Powell didn’t provide a timeline for ending balance sheet runoff.

The Fed chair also hinted that more interest rate cuts are in the cards. He also alluded to the tightrope the central bank is trying to walk.

“If we move too quickly, then we may leave the inflation job unfinished and have to come back later and finish it. If we move too slowly, there may be unnecessary losses, painful losses, in the employment market. So, we’re in the difficult situation of balancing those two things.”

Fed Balance Sheet Operations Explained

Quantitative Easing

Interest rate policy gets the most attention, but the Federal Reserve balance sheet is an important monetary policy tool. In effect, it serves as a direct pipeline to the money supply.

When the Fed buys assets – primarily U.S. Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities – it does so with money created out of thin air. Those assets go on the balance sheet, and the new money gets injected into the financial system and ultimately the broader economy. This process is known as quantitative easing (QE).

Before the 2008 financial crisis and Great Recession, the balance sheet was just over $900 billion. By the end of the pandemic era, it stood at just under $9 trillion. In other words, the Fed pumped over $8 trillion into the economy in 14 years through QE.

We can see the impact of QE in the trajectory of the money supply. Note that it declined after the Fed began QT and cut interest rates in 2022, but it has since resumed its relentless climb.

When Ben Bernanke launched the first round of QE at the onset of the Great Recession, he assured Congress that the Fed was not monetizing the debt. (When the Fed buys a bond with money created out of thin air, it is effectively turning that debt into money - thus the term "debt monetization.")

He said the difference between debt monetization and the Fed’s policy was that the central bank was not providing a permanent source of financing. He said the Treasuries would only remain on the Fed’s balance sheet temporarily.

He assured Congress that once the crisis was over, the Federal Reserve would sell the bonds it bought during the emergency. That never happened. And then the Fed doubled down, expanding the balance sheet by nearly $5 trillion during the pandemic. This is, by definition, inflation.

As former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh explained in an op-ed published by the Wall Street Journal last year, there is a direct connection between this monetary expansion and price inflation.

“The monetary base is up 60 percent since the pandemic. Another measure of money, M2, is up 36 percent in the past four years. The inflation surge in the same period– cumulatively about 22 percent– shouldn’t have been a surprise.”

Quantitative Tightening

Quantitative tightening unwinds quantitative easing. As Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities mature, the central bank doesn’t replace them, thereby shrinking the size of the balance sheet over time.

Since the Fed isn’t replacing the bonds, the U.S. Treasury must raise money from the public to redeem the central bank. Of course, Uncle Sam is broke. That means the Treasury Department has to sell a new bond on the open market – essentially borrowing money to pay off the old debt.

When a private individual or entity buys a Treasury, they typically use cash, reducing their bank deposit. In effect, this lowers overall financial system reserves and shifts more debt financing to the public.

The Fed announced a balance sheet reduction plan in March 2022 when it could no longer convince everybody that price inflation was “transitory.” The plan wasn’t exactly ambitious, given the amount of inflation it created during the pandemic.

If the Fed followed the blueprint (and it didn’t), it would take 7.8 years for the Fed to shrink its balance sheet back to pre-pandemic levels. This doesn't even account for the trillions added in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis.

And of course, the central bank didn’t stick with the plan. The Fed slowed the pace of balance sheet reduction in May 2024. From its peak, the Fed has run around $2.4 trillion off the balance sheet. That sounds impressive, but it only represents about half of the increase during the pandemic alone.

Powell said that the Fed has no plans to reduce the balance sheet back to pre-pandemic levels. So much for Bernanke’s promise.

Why End QT Now?

If expanding the money supply is inflationary, it logically follows that to truly beat down price inflation, the Fed needed to shrink the balance sheet substantially. Theoretically, it should have reduced the size of the balance sheet at least to the neighborhood of pre-crisis levels, as Bernanke promised.

Rate hikes aren’t enough to wring all the liquidity out of the economy. Warsh made this very point in his op-ed, explaining that if the Fed is serious about taming inflation, it needs to significantly reduce the size of the balance sheet. In other words, it needs to undo the money creation of the past decade-plus.

It didn’t. And it won’t. So, if inflation isn’t at the 2 percent target, why did the Fed start winding down its balance sheet reduction in June? And why are they talking about ending it altogether today?

Probably because they learned their lesson in 2018. After the 2008 financial crisis, the Fed maintained its bloated balance sheet until 2017.

When it finally made an effort to reduce the balance sheet, it didn’t last very long. Why? Because the monetary tightening was about to break the economy and the financial system.

The stock market crashed in the fall of 2018, and there were recession warning signs flashing in the economy. The Fed put rate hikes on hold in December that year and started cutting rates in 2019.

In August 2019, the Fed reversed course on balance sheet reduction and launched a new round of quantitative easing as the short-term lending markets went haywire. Fed officials refused to call it QE. They even denied that it was QE. But whatever you want to call it, the result was the same as QE. The balance sheet began to expand again.

The Fed was able to shrink the balance sheet more this time around because the monetary expansion was bigger and faster during the pandemic than it was in 2008. But the central bankers know they’re playing with fire. They know that this economy can’t run without easy money. And that’s exactly why they’ve already slowed their roll even though they continue to claim inflation isn’t dead yet.

The National Debt Problem

While Powell & company would never admit it, the central bank has no choice but to end balance sheet reduction due to the federal government’s borrowing and spending problem. When Bernanke claimed the central bank wasn’t monetizing the debt, he was either hopelessly naive or he was lying.

In effect, the Fed supports the government’s borrowing by creating artificial demand for Treasuries. This drives the price higher and yields lower, driving the U.S. government's borrowing costs down.

If the Fed weren’t holding so much U.S. debt, Treasury interest rates would be substantially higher. An end to QT makes sense given that the federal government is paying more than $1 trillion in interest expense right now.

While an end to balance sheet reduction won’t inject newly created money into the economy, it will reduce the number of bonds the government has to sell on the open market, thereby easing supply, raising Treasury prices, and putting downward pressure on yields.

Simply ending balance sheet reduction might not be enough. Given falling global demand for Treasuries and persistently high yields, the central bank may well have to launch quantitative easing again in the near future to press its thumb harder on the Treasury market. In effect, this would be a return to money printing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ultragenyx: Rough Road Ahead Despite Growing Revenues And Catalysts
data.description:
This is my third Ultragenyx (NASDAQ:RARE) article, following 03/2024's "Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical Earnings: No Visible Path To Financial Success Yet". Ultragenx has had a rough go since the publication of No Visible Path, with its price since knocked down ~40%.

Ultragenyx has built a mix of approved and late stage molecules over its 15 year existence

Overview

In its latest 10-K Ultragenyx includes the following graphic listing its four approved molecules and its six in clinical development:

Its 10-K sets out the following table of disaggregated total revenues which it has generated in recent years from its four approved molecules:

Slide 32 from Ultragenyx's 09/2025 website Corporate Presentation provides a graphic presentation of its approved therapies' revenues over the years along with its guidance for 2025:

CRYSVITA as will be discussed in greater detail below is its crucial cash cow. In addition it has its three other approved therapies two of which are in solid growth mode. In addition to reviewing its approved therapies I also look into its most advanced projects in development.

CRYSVITA

In 2018, Ultragenyx and its collaborator Kyowa Kirin (OTCPK:KYKOF) announced FDA approval of CRYSVITA (burosumab-twza) for the treatment of X-linked hypophosphatemia [XLH] in adult and pediatric patients one year of age and older. Subsequently in 2020 CRYSVITA was approved to treat certain instances of fibroblast growth factor 23 [FGF23]-related hypophosphatemia in tumor-induced osteomalacia [TIO] associated with phosphaturic mesenchymal tumors.

Ultragenyx's annual CRYSVITA product sales revenues as reported in its 10-K's have nearly doubled every year since 2020's sales of ~$10,000. The trajectory of other CRYSVITA revenue categories variously referred to as CRYSVITA royalty revenue and CRYSVITA collaboration revenue in Profit-Share Territory is more difficult to assess.

As shown above in 2024 CRYSVITA collaboration revenue in Profit-Share Territory fell to nil from ~$71 million in 2023 and ~$215 million in 2022. Its table below of disaggregated revenue from its 10-K issued in 2023 shows significant revenues of this type generated during years 2020-2022:

During its Q2/2025 earnings Call CFO Horn reiterated its prior revenue guidance indicating that:

.... CRYSVITA revenue is expected to be between $460 million and $480 million, which includes all regions and all forms of CRYSVITA revenue to Ultragenyx. This range represents 12% to 17% growth over 2024. (emphasis added)

Bottom line is that CRYSVITA should remain in growth mode for the balance of 2025. Its future trajectory will depend on a variety of factors most significantly its competitive matrix and its intellectual property [IP] protection.

In terms of competition its 10-K advises as follows:

With respect to CRYSVITA, although we are not aware of any other products currently in clinical development by a competitor for the treatment of XLH and TIO, it is possible that competitors may produce, develop, and commercialize therapeutics, or utilize other approaches such as gene therapy, to treat XLH and TIO. Most pediatric patients with XLH are managed using oral phosphate replacement and/or vitamin D therapy, which is relatively inexpensive and therefore may adversely affect our ability to commercialize CRYSVITA, if approved, in some countries.

This excerpt indicates that the coast appears clear for CRYSVITA in terms other disease modifying therapies over the near term. As for CRYSVITA's IP protection, Presentation Slide 40 provides a quick overview:

CRYSVITA gradually loses pieces of its protective matrix over the next 10 years. If I am correct in my assumption that the "Crysvita CoM patent" expiring in 2032 references its composition of matter patent, It appears to have strong colorable IP protection until then.

DOJOLVI

DOJOLVI (UX007/triheptanoin) is the second largest contributor to Ultragenyx's revenues, albeit it is guided to provide a mere fraction of Crysvita's aggregated revenues. The FDA approved DOJOLVI in 2020 as a source of calories and fatty acids for the treatment of pediatric and adult patients with confirmed long-chain fatty acid oxidation disorders (LC-FAOD).

At the time it was the first FDA approved treatment for LC-FAOD. DOJOLVI's current competitive situation is complex as described in Ultragenyx's 10-K. Most concerning are a raft of filed abbreviated new drug applications [ANDAs] seeking FDA approval to market a generic version of Dojolvi.

Based on DOJOLVI's exclusivity profile shown in Presentation slide 41 with its Dojolvi CoM Patent expiring in 2029, I would expect that Ultragenyx could forestall any such efforts to market a generic until at least 2029.

EVKEEZA

EVKEEZA (evinacumab-dgnb) was developed by Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (RGNX). Its FDA label shows that its initial U.S. approval came in 2021 for use by adults and children aged >12 years; it is an adjunct to other low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol [LDL-C] lowering therapies for the treatment of homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia [HoFH]. Subsequently, it was approved by the FDA for children aged 5 to 11 in 2023 and then in 09/2025 for children from age 1 to less than 5 years old.

In 01/2022, Ultragenyx announced an in-license with Regeneron to commercialize EVKEEZA outside of the U.S. It then began the process of engaging country authorities within the EEA, Latin America, Canada and Japan to negotiate pricing and reimbursement guidelines.

The general terms of the license are stated in the extract below from Presentation slide 37:

During the Call CCO Harris reported as follows on EVKEEZA which he noted had only received reimbursement approval outside the U.S. in the last couple of years:

...In the EMEA region, we now have patients on reimbursed therapy from nearly all of the major countries, and we have added almost 100 patients since the beginning of the year with a total of approximately 285 patients across 15 countries. We continue to successfully navigate the country-by-country pricing negotiations and respond to named patient treatment requests across the whole EMEA region.

Our team in Japan continues to build on the launch momentum following the pricing and reimbursement approval that we received last year. In Canada, we are continuing pricing negotiations with government health authorities and recently added younger pediatric patients to the label. Over time, we expect Evkeeza revenue to contribute more meaningfully to the total revenue as we continue to successfully launch this transformative product for HoFH patients with its high potency due to a novel mechanism of action regardless of background therapy.

MEPSEVII

In terms of revenue generation MEPSEVII comes in nearly tied with EVKEEZA as a distant fourth. MEPSEVII (vestronidase alfa), was the first medicine approved for the treatment of children and adults with Mucopolysaccharidosis VII (MPS VII, Sly syndrome) in 2017.

In terms of competition, the 10-K is unable to cite any other compounds currently in clinical development for MPS VII. Slide 42 cites a MEPSEVII CoM Patent expiring in 2035.

As noted by CMO Crombez during the 09/2025 Morgan Stanley 23rd Annual Global Healthcare Conference, it is CRYSVITA not EVKEEZA or MEPSEVII which are driving the growth at Ultragenyx.

DTX401

Presentation slide 25 below provides a quick snapshot of Ultragenyx's next shot at an approved therapy:

DTX401 is slated for submission of its BLA to the FDA later in 2025 for treatment of glycogen storage disease type Ia [GSDIa], with launch expected in 2026. True to Ultragenyx's ticker symbol, GSDIa is quite rare with is ~6,000 patient prevalence in commercially accessible geographies.

The 10-K reveals a wide open near term competitive landscape for DTX401 as follows:

With respect to DTX401, there are currently no pharmacologic treatments for patients with GSDIa. We are aware of an mRNA therapy, mRNA-3745, in Phase 1 for GSDIa by Moderna.

As for DTX401's exclusivity, the 10-K notes:

We have a non-exclusive license from the National Institutes of Health, or NIH, to an issued U.S. patent expiring in 2034 (not accounting for any available PTE) and corresponding foreign patents covering a recombinant nucleic acid construct used in DTX401 that includes a codon-optimized version of the G6Pase gene.

Lacking patent exclusivity DTX401 Ultragenyx must rely on its orphan drug exclusivity for protection from generics.

UX111

In 12/2024 Ultragenyx filed its BLA for UX111 (ABO-102) AAV gene therapy as a treatment for patients with Sanfilippo syndrome type A [MPS IIIA]. In 07/2025 the FDA rejected its application with a CRL. The company's 07/2025 release announcing the CRL advised that FDA's referenced CMC issues were:

... readily addressable and many have already been addressed. While the CRL will delay the potential approval of UX111 to 2026, we are working with urgency to respond and resubmit...

Unfortunately the CRL is not only impacting UX111. It is also causing delay for DTX401 as noted by CMO Crombez during the Call where he noted:

... We want to proactively resolve any relevant CMC and facility questions that derive from the UX111 CRL that could possibly impact the DTX401 submission before we finalize the CMC section of the BLA. We expect to resolve the observations over the next few months and then finalize the BLA submission.

Ultragenyx has significant liquidity but only a modest cash runway

As stated during the Call as of 06/30/2025, Ultragenyx had $539 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities; this included $80 million of net proceeds raised through its ATM facility.

The 10-K sets out Ultragenyx's imposing totals of cash burn as reflected by its annual net cash used in operating activities totaling $380,465 for 2022, $474,806 for 2023 and $414,188 for 2024. Its cash burn for H1/2025 shown in its 10-Q is $274,748. This suggests that its cash runway beyond H2/2025 will be ~two quarters. In other words investors should expect ongoing financings.

If Ultragenyx can manage to secure approval of UX111, DTX401, and/or UX143 before the program sunsets in 09/2026, it will earn cash relief by monetizing priority review vouchers [PRV] as planned per Presentation slide 33. Each PRV it can cash in should extend its cash runway by ~quarter.
